title: Autocorrelation-based Fiducial Markers for Traceability
abstract: Classical approaches to the rectification of a single image of a product, without stereo correspondences, require spatial landmarks. These landmarks, constructed from high-contrast elementary shapes that can be detected with simple algorithms, are highly conspicuous. To rectify complex deformations, one can use chessboard patterns of markers with elements that break quadrilateral symmetry, such as the three eyes of a QR code. However, these marker boards are even more conspicuous than a single marker. In traceability applications, only one site of marking is used, limiting the complexity of the surface on which it can be read, and exposing the mark to deidentification attacks for diversion of the product to a grey market. We introduce a method for constructing stealth and robust fiducial markers that can be displayed across a surface, limiting exposure to marker tampering for product deidentification. These markers, which we refer to as self-rectifying textures, can be used to rectify complex deformations by solving an inverse problem rather than relying on pixel correspondences of conspicuous landmarks. These stealth textures place fiducial markers in the autocorrelation of the image. In this way, crops of the deformed texture can be rectified using only these spatially invariant statistical properties. Affine transformations of an image correspond to linear transformations of the autocorrelation, without phase component. Exploiting this fact, self-rectifying textures enable the local estimation of the differential of a planar deformation by identifying landmarks in the autocorrelation image, such as peaks, whose locations in the fronto-parallel view of the texture are known. The translation component can be recovered independently via phase correlation. A rectifying map, modulo translations, can also be fit directly to local observations of the differential of the deformation, without access to the rectified texture or need for phase correlation. Self-rectifying textures can be used for communication, watermarking, authentication, surface identification, calibration, and geometry processing.

title: Framework for a Hybrid McEliece–HQC Cryptosystem for Compact Public Keys and Efficient Decapsulation
abstract: Post-quantum cryptography has come to light in the digital world as it is essential for securing digital systems against quantum attacks. Recent trends show code-based cryptosystems like McEliece and HQC among the best options as they demonstrate promising security. However, these schemes still fall short on certain parameters. HQC brings slower decoding due to lack of effective algebraic algorithms whereas McEliece demands very large public keys limiting its practicality in memory-constrained environments. Prior attempts to reduce key size included QC-MDPC McEliece or BIKE. These attempts included altering the underlying code family, but this led to the sacrifice of either security or efficiency. However, little to no attention has been given to simultaneously rethinking the error model used in code-based systems, which could open up new trade-offs in security and performance.This paper proposes a hybrid McEliece–HQC cryptosystem that integrates the structural compactness of quasi-cyclic HQC with the robust decoding of classic McEliece. It establishes a theoretical foundation for a hybrid McEliece–HQC cryptosystem that combines quasi-cyclic compactness with Goppa decoding efficiency, laying the groundwork for future implementation and validation.

title: Design of Polar Subcodes for Permutation Decoding
abstract: A novel construction of randomized polar subcodes and subcodes of extended BCH codes is presented. The proposed approach stems from a new heuristic for building polar code frozen set which minimizes the error probability of successive cancellation algorithm applied to permuted noisy codewords. The frame error rate (FER) of the proposed codes under permutation-based decoding is comparable to FER of existing polar subcodes under successive cancellation list or sequential algorithm.

title: A New NB-LDPC Code Construction Method With Arbitrary Generalized Girth
abstract: Non-binary low density parity check (NB-LDPC) codes are known to have higher correction capability than their binary counterparts, low density parity check (LDPC) codes. The latest technique for analyzing NB-LDPC codes is to measure generalized girth. The generalized girth cannot be measured in a binary LDPC code, and can be considered an indicator for measuring the performance of NB-LDPC code. In this paper, we propose a new analysis method to represent NB-LDPC code as a graph. This graph is called a permutation graph. In this paper, we present and prove a reasoning for analyzing generalized girth using the permutation graph. This is a faster and simpler method than the existing generalized girth analysis method. The proposed code construction algorithm constructs NB-LDPC codes to have an arbitrary generalized girth length. Because the permutation graph-based analysis is applied, which is more effective than existing methods, the complexity of code construction is significantly reduced. This enables more efficient code construction. The decoding simulations show that the proposed method improves the correction performance of NB-LDPC codes compared to the existing state-of-the-art.

title: Generative Semantic Communication for AI Model Parameters
abstract: In the future network, delivering AI models from the edge to terminals is crucial for low-latency, privacy-preserving applications. However, this process poses significant challenges in terms of transmission efficiency and the adaptability across heterogeneous terminals. To address these issues, this paper proposes a semantic-empowered model parameters transmission and generation scheme named ParaSC, which leverages the semantic characteristics of model parameters to reduce communication cost and improve system adaptability. Specifically, ParaSC designs a semantic transmitter to compress model parameters into parameter semantic symbols (PSS). Then, when a terminal requests a model, the system conducts a matching check against the existing PSSs. If matched, only the PSS is transmitted to the terminal, where a pre-deployed semantic receiver reconstructs it into model parameters. Moreover, for unmatched cases, ParaSC extends PSSs to generative purposes and proposes a model parameter generation framework based on generative adversarial network (GAN). This framework employs an optimized GAN generator to construct the model base (MB), which is defined as a synthesized semantic representation that integrates the feature extraction capabilities of multiple models. After transmitting MB to the terminal, the terminal reconstructs it into generated model parameters and adapts to the target task via lightweight fine-tuning. Experiments show that ParaSC reduces bandwidth by 10%–43.3% compared to baselines, adapts to SNR variations without cliff effects, and saves 42.8%–50% computational resources over transfer learning.

title: Guest Editorial: Special Issue on Key Technologies Towards Standardization of 6G and NextG
Soft-Information LLM Fusion for LDPC-Coded Text Over Visible-Light Links
abstract: This letter proposes an LDPC–LLM–LDPC receiver in which black-box language-model confidence is converted to pseudo-LLRs and fused within belief-propagation decoding, so accepted suggestions remain parity-constrained. Reliability-guided masking, vote-based confidence gating, and adaptive LLR-scale matching control when and how strongly the LLM contributes. Experiments on an OLED-based VLC testbed, using 4-bit Qwen3-4B-Instruct over nine operating points show consistent BER reduction. Compared with text-domain LLM correction, the LLR-domain pipeline better preserves bit-level and semantic fidelity, confirmed by CER and Sentence-BERT similarity.

title: Learning to Decode: Graph Attention Network-Gated Recurrent Unit as Channel Decoder
abstract: Training deep learning (DL) models to address channel coding tasks has drawn increasing attention, a paradigm referred to as “learning to decode”. By constructing Tanner graphs based on parity-check matrices, graph neural networks (GNNs) have shown great promise, yet their performance can be further enhanced with more sophisticated neural architectures. This letter proposes a novel GNN-based decoder that enhances the traditional message-passing framework through the integration of a graph attention network (GAT) and a gated recurrent unit (GRU), termed GAT-GRU. Extensive experiments validate that the proposed GAT-GRU decoder outperforms conventional belief propagation (BP), weighted BP (WBP), and GNN decoders across Bose-Chaudhuri-Hocquenghem (BCH), regular low-density parity-check (LDPC), and 5G NR LDPC codes. Furthermore, GAT-GRU exhibits robust scalability to unseen code lengths and code rates, particularly for LDPC codes.

title: Spatially Coupled Convolutional Codes via Block-Oriented Accumulation: Analysis and Quantized Decoding
abstract: Construction of high-performance spatially coupled codes has received considerable attention recently. This paper presents a new construction of spatially coupled convolutional codes (SC-CC), in which the codewords of the underlying convolutional codes are coupled together with the block-oriented accumulator (BA). The resulting codes are referred to as the BA-based SC-CCs (BA-SC-CCs). First, we employ the extrinsic information transfer (EXIT) chart analysis to study the iterative decoding thresholds of BA-SC-CCs. Our analysis indicates that, with appropriate component convolutional codes, the iterative decoding thresholds of BA-SC-CCs can be within 0.1 dB of the channel capacities. Second, we prove that the free distance of a BA-SC-CC is greater than that of the underlying convolutional code. Third, we present a method to analyze the decoding error propagation phenomenon of BA-SC-CCs. To enhance the decoding performance, we finally introduce a quantized threshold-based Max-Log-MAP (T-Max-Log-MAP) decoder for CCs, which can be incorporated into the decoder of BA-SC-CCs. Extensive numerical results demonstrate the advantages of the proposed codes and the new quantized decoder. Specifically, terminated BA-SC-CCs perform comparably to the braided convolutional codes (BCC) and outperform regular spatially coupled low-density parity-check (SC-LDPC) codes and spatially coupled repeater-convolutional-combiner (SC-RCC) codes. Furthermore, the proposed quantized decoder performs better than the conventional quantized decoder.

title: Binary Deterministic Sensing Matrix Construction Using Manifold Optimization
abstract: Binary deterministic sensing matrices are attractive for sampling sparse signals due to their reliance only on sum operations for generating measurement vectors. Additionally, their sparsity facilitates the use of low-complexity reconstruction algorithms. In this paper, we present a novel approach for constructing low-density binary deterministic sensing matrices by formulating an optimization problem on the statistical manifold. Unlike many existing methods, the proposed construction supports arbitrary matrix sizes, offering greater flexibility. We also establish the convergence of the proposed algorithm. The resulting matrices exhibit low coherence and regular column weight. Simulation results demonstrate that our method outperforms existing approaches in terms of reconstruction accuracy.
